Email deliverability can be a black box. Why do some emails end up in the spam folder and others in the inbox? While we regularly share deliverability tips one at a time, in this video, we're going to cover 10 pro tips for landing in the inbox. You ready? Let's go. First, the domain you send emails from can significantly impact deliverability.

Generally speaking, you should send from a subdomain instead of your root domain. Sending from a subdomain helps you communicate the email's purpose and can limit any reputation damage to that subdomain. If you use subdomains, you can separate your reputation. In other words, if there's an issue with the subdomain you use for marketing emails, it's less likely to impact your transactional subdomain.

DMARC is an email authentication protocol that tells mail servers what to do if an email fails SPF and DKIM. In this way, it protects against email spoofing. A DMARC record builds trust with mailbox providers as it allows them to verify that your emails are authorized by your domain. We've linked guides below, but let us know if a full tutorial would be helpful.

Third, ensure that the URLs and the body of your email match the sending domain. Mismatched URLs can trigger spam filters since spammers often use this technique. Any images should also match the sending domain. Next, avoid link and open tracking in transactional emails like notifications, magic links, and more. Both can be great for marketing emails but not for transactional ones.

It can actually damage your deliverability. Setting up separate subdomains for transactional and marketing emails can often make it easier to include or remove these practices for different email types. Next, keep emails small and accessible. Gmail, for instance, has a size limit of 102 kilobytes for each email message. Once that limit is reached, the remaining content is clipped.

So avoid bloated emails or emails with a lot of images. Using a plain text version of your email is also good practice. This ensures that your message is accessible to all recipients, including those who have email clients that don't support HTML. Spammers often fish from cousin or lookalike domains, so avoid using them.

Instead of registering an email-specific domain, which can cause brand confusion and look like spam, separate your sending using the subdomains method we covered earlier. When testing, it's tempting to send to fake email addresses to test for bounces, among other things. While testing is important, intentionally hard-bouncing emails can impact deliverability. Because while the emails are fake, the bounces are very real and will damage your reputation.

Instead, you should use a testing email address provided by your email service or create a testing email account. Testing goes together with this next tip, list hygiene. Simply put, only send to those who've asked to be sent to, and don't send to unsubscribers, those who haven't engaged with your content, or those who've marked your previous emails as spam.

Testing should account for all these scenarios. To keep a clean email list, you can capture bounces or spam complaints using a webhook and remove those email addresses from your list. Ninth, don't use no-reply emails as this signals that communication is one-way and diminishes trust with email providers. It tells inboxes that they cannot provide feedback on your emails, like reporting spam.

Finally, mailbox providers are suspicious when you suddenly change the volume of sending. If you want to send millions of emails in a few months, you need to warm up your domain by sending them regularly ahead of time. Otherwise, you risk a large bounce rate and a big hit to your reputation, which can be hard to recover from in the future.

you kind of burn out and what that causes also is you're just inefficient in terms of the stuff the project that you're working on. Maybe you get a little bit of tunnel vision too that can create a lot of problems. So those are the two big things that can derail a a really good plan.

Every subscription business deals with failed payments. A card expires, a bank flags a transaction, a customer forgets to update their billing info. It happens constantly, and most of the time it has nothing to do with whether they actually want to keep using your product. The problem is, if you don't act fast and act smart, that temporary billing hiccup turns into a lost customer.

And lost customers are expensive to replace. Most teams send a generic your payment failed email and hope the customer notices. Maybe they do, maybe they don't. The average email open rate sits around 20%, which means most people never even see it. That is a lot of revenue quietly slipping out the door. Churnkey's dunning campaigns are built to fix exactly that.

Instead of blasting the same message to everyone, you get a full omni-channel recovery system that treats each customer as an individual. Different languages, different time zones, different plans, different payment methods. Every message is personal, relevant, and timed to actually reach people. It starts with email. You send from your own verified domain, which improves deliverability and builds trust.

You pipe in your customer data to create targeted segments based on plan type, subscription length, or any other attribute that matters to your business. And you can push copy changes instantly. No engineering required. When email isn't enough, SMS picks up the slack. Open rates on SMS sit at 98% a massive jump from standard email.

That difference is significant when you're trying to recover real revenue. Churnkey provisions local numbers for your customer base and includes one-tap payment links that go to a mobile-optimized checkout page. The less friction between the customer and updating their card, the better. For customers who are still inside your product, there's the payment recovery wall.

A tasteful in-app message that prompts them to update their payment details right then and there. No page loads, no clicking away. You can configure how soft or hard you want it to be, restrict access to certain features, or display it across your entire experience. And for B2B products with multiple seats, there is the billing contacts API.

It lets you route recovery messages to the right person, the billing admin, the account owner, whoever actually controls the payment method. No more recovery emails landing in the wrong inbox. Together, these channels recover up to 89% of failed payments. That is not a rounding error. For a growing business, payment failures typically account for massive losses every year.

Getting that back with an automated system that runs without your team lifting a finger is about as clean a win as retention gets. Setting it up takes one click to connect your payment processor. TurnKey handles the campaign infrastructure, the segmentation, the timing, and the optimization. You can customize as much or as little as you want.

If failed payments are quietly eating into your revenue, dunning campaigns are the fastest thing you can fix. Head to turnkey.co to see how much you could be recovering.

Do you know what drives me absolutely up the wall? It's when a company proudly announces that they're building a community and then proceeds to open a bloody Discord server. That's not a community. That's a slightly disorganized chat room full of your most excitable users probably arguing about emojis and light mode. Now, don't get [music] me wrong, Discord, Slack, whatever you fancy, they all have their place.

But if your entire community strategy lives inside of one of those platforms, well, you're playing the world's smallest symphony for the top 20% of people who are probably already obsessed with you to begin with. By the end of this video, you're going to know [music] exactly how to build a thriving movement around your company, your product, your project, one that goes way beyond Discord.

And I'm going to do it in just [music] five steps. And once you see how easy this is, you're never going to look at community building quite the same way again. Now, [music] this is the key principle. A community isn't a platform. It's a belief system. Platforms are where people talk. Movements are why they care.

Most companies treat their community a bit like a marketing channel. But the companies that really win, GitHub, Notion, Stripe, Figma, they build them around a creed [music] and not just a chat room. Just look at GitHub versus Atlassian. Alassian is brilliant, right? Jira, Bitbucket, Confluence are all well-engineered, well-funded products built by great people.

But GitHub owns developer mind share. Why? Because GitHub showed up with a set of principles that really resonated with developers. Openness, social coding, collaboration without the corporate They didn't just build a tool. They built a tribe around the idea that coding should be social, accessible, and human. All right, so let's break this down.

So step one is that you need to define your creed, your villains, and your rituals. At stage shift, when we help companies do this, we start by figuring out three really important things. One, what are your guiding principles? What are the values the audience already believes in, but you can articulate better than anybody else?

Two, who are your non-believers? Who are the people who do it the wrong way and represent everything that you are rebelling against? [music] And three, what are your rituals? What are the repeatable actions that people can participate in that reinforce those principles we defined earlier on? For example, GitHub's creed was all about collaboration and transparency.

Their non-believers were old school software teams hiding behind firewalls. Their rituals were forks, pull requests, and meetups, real tangible interactions that really reinforce that sense of belonging with developers. If you can define those three things, you have laid the foundation for your movement. Now, step number two is don't start with a platform.

Start with an event. You see, before you ever open up a forum or spin up a Slack workspace or a Discord channel, gather people together, even if it's just 10 of them, into a live environment where they can share, teach, and learn from each other. Humans connect through energy and body language, not just emoji reactions.

When we run events with State Shift, whether they're online or in person, the first 5 minutes is kind of a little bit chaotic and intentionally so. You see, we hand participants a shared dock in our Zoom sessions, for example, and we say, "Tell us what you want to get out of this session.

We co-create the agenda together in real time." And that's how you get really amazing engagement by giving people a voice where they have control of their destiny. Even if it's just a live session, you see, a good event isn't a PowerPoint hostage situation with one person rambling on for 45 minutes with a bit of Q&A at the end.

It's a structured jam session. Right? The goal of every event, whether physical or digital, should be that everyone leaves having shared something of value. That's what really creates that kind of stickiness. Oh, and if you're finding this helpful, I would love if you could hit that like and subscribe button. It really helps make a huge difference for small channels like mine and helps me to keep putting out stuff that isn't just fluff and hopefully get seen by other people.

All right, so step three is to build the lightest possible digital home. So, this is where the Discord piece kind of comes in. You see, once your people have met each other and shared real value, then you give them somewhere to hang out between events. It does really doesn't matter what platform this is.

It could be Discord, it could be Slack, LinkedIn, a WhatsApp group, even a mailing list. It all works if it's familiar and comfortable for your audience. For example, Notion didn't just dump everyone into a Slack. They let their fans self-organize wherever they hang out, whether it's Reddit, Facebook groups, X. Their official Facebook group, for example, has hundreds of thousands of members.

And the company kind of lightly curates amplifies community posts rather than just overengineering a single platform. Now, this choice of platform really depends on how your audience likes to jam with other people online. Developers, they love Discord. Executives, not a chance. I've seen Fortune 500 executives break out into hives at the mere mention of joining a Discord.

So, choose the platform that your audience already uses, not the one that you think is kind of trendy and cool. Then, what you do is you seed your platform with what we call conversation stars. These are two or three conversations that you kick off every week that are designed to get people engaged and get people participating.

They should be relevant and always like really easy for people to respond to. For example, if you're building a photography product, ask your users to share what their favorite lenses are. If you're building a developer platform, ask them which feature would save them the most time in this quarter. Right? Keep it really lightweight.

The goal is momentum, not message board philosophy debates. Now, step number four is to master the awkward small talk pattern. You see, when somebody actually replies inside of your community platform, don't just fire off a thumbs up emoji and vanish. You got to engage. Ask follow-ups. Make introductions. If two people care about the same thing, connect them.

This is why we call it the awkward small talk plan because we want to kind of keep it going over and over again, right? It's a little bit like when you host a party at your house, right? If somebody arrives at your house early, you don't just ignore them. You introduce them to another guest and try and find the common ground that they've got.

Oh, you both build in Typescript and drink industrial quantities of coffee. Brilliant. You should get to know each other. And that's the essence of great community leadership. It's all about orchestrating connection. When we work with our customers at State Shift, we train our customers to spot these little micro moments and turn them into those kinds of rituals because every connection that you broker is just another thread in the fabric of your overall movement.

And set number five is to measure everything within reason and iterate relentlessly. You see, building a movement really isn't about grand speeches. It's about tuning an engine. Measure participation, retention, and outcomes. For your events, track how many people show up and how long they stay for. For your online community, track your DAU, MAU, that's your daily active users divided by monthly active users.

It gives you kind of a heartbeat of engagement. Then every single week, just ask one question. How can we make this a little bit better? And that's it. That's the secret. You measure, you hypothesize, you implement, and you repeat. Do it over and over and over again. And look, I know this can feel messy at first.

You're going to have low turnout at some of your events. You're going to have crickets in your chat rooms, maybe even a few skeptics who think that community is all fluffy nonsense. Ignore them. The companies that really stick with it, that keep refining and keep measuring and keep showing up are the ones that really dominate the market and build the most incredible communities out there.

If you want proof, just look at companies like Versel and Figma. Both built their user bases not through advertising blitzes, but through those rituals, templates, challenges, showcases that really let users see themselves as part of something much bigger than themselves. GitHub didn't buy ads to become a verb. They created a movement around contribution.

And that's the power that you're aiming for. So, let's wrap this up. So, if you want to build an amazing movement that goes way beyond Discord, one, define your creed, your non-believers, and your rituals. Two, start with an event, not a platform. Three, choose the simplest possible digital home that fits your audience.

Four, master the art of that awkward small talk. Connection is everything. And then five, measure, iterate, [music] and build your flywheel. If you follow these steps, you won't just have a chat room, you'll have a real movement that really grows itself. And if you want help doing this faster, that's literally what we do at State Shift.

We work with tech companies to install systems just like this. Frameworks, metrics, rituals and flywheels, all so they can go from scattered [music] activity to really predictable growth. So with a lot of usability testing and serving and the more that you can diagnose the problem, then you come up with ideas maybe if we tried it this way, maybe if we tried it that way and those new additional things can can make a huge difference. So there's a great quote from Amazon's uh founder, former CEO where he said, "Our success in Amazon is a function of how many experiments we run per day, per month, per year." And so volume of experiments really starts to matter.

And when we talk about the growth hacking process, it's it's uh it starts with analysis. And so that's really the first analysis I'm doing is is more of a qualitative analysis of who loves the product and why do they love it. So the first thing that I do when when I validated product market fit is I figure out why do I have product market fit?

Because if I don't understand why I have product market fit, you know, then I'm not going to be able to grow very well. Let's say I think that people love the product for this reason, but they really love it for this reason. All of my advertisements, all of my messaging is pushing them to do something with the product that it's bad at doing, then then I'm probably not going to keep those people.

It's really important if you have product market fit to to understand what is the main benefit that those must-have users are having. So it's really hard to improve something you don't understand. So that's where the analysis starts. Then you start to say okay I have ideas to improve this situation. And so that's the next step is to generate some ideas.

The next step is to actually prioritize which of those ideas you want to test first. Then you start to run the tests. Then you analyze the results. And it's just that that repeating process of you need to try a lot of ideas to figure out what's going to work and what's not going to work.

And so one of the most important things in growth hacking is just running a lot of tests, a high velocity of tests. I'm going to emphasize that over and over because it's really important. And I think what we realized was that nobody knew consistently what was going to be the best. And so the only way to figure out what was the best was just to test a lot of things.

Uh, a great analogy is is one that comes from the sports world where you have there's a quote from a a hockey player Wayne Gretzky that essentially says you miss every shot that you don't take. So, but that also applies to soccer uh or or football as they call it in most of the world.

But essentially, if you only take one shot, maybe you make it. But if you take 10 shots in a game or 50 shots in a game, you're much more likely to win because every shot is an opportunity to score. And it's the same thing with testing. Every test you run is an opportunity to find that one big test that ultimately is a lot more successful than everything else that you do.

And so, it's a mistake often when teams are obsessed on trying to run one perfect test. they get so excited about an idea that they they only want to focus on one. It's much better to to make sure that every week we're running three different tests every single week or five different tests every single week depending on the size of the company.
